title: Making conclusions in a z test for a proportion
description: Make conclusions in a one-sample z test for a proportion based on the P-value and significance level.
topic_path: Concluding a test for a population proportion
Making conclusions in a z test for a proportion

assessment_items.question_content:
A public opinion survey investigated whether a majority (more than $50\%$) of adults supported a tax increase to help fund the local school system. A random sample of $200$ adults showed that $113$ of those sampled supported the tax increase.

Researchers used these results to test $H_0:p=0.5$ versus $H_\text{a} : p>0.5$, where $p$ is the true proportion of adults that support the tax increase. They calculated a test statistic of $z\approx 1.84$ and a corresponding P-value of approximately $0.033$.

**Assuming the conditions for inference were met, which of these is an appropriate conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

The P-value in this problem is $\blueD{0.033}$.

If $\alpha=\purpleD{0.01}$, then we have $\blueD{0.033} \geq \purpleD{0.01}$, in which case they would fail to reject $H_0$.

If $\alpha=\goldD{0.05}$, then we have $\blueD{0.033} < \goldD{0.05}$, in which case they would reject $H_0$ and accept $H_\text{a}$.

In this context, accepting $H_\text{a} : p>0.5$ is equivalent to saying that more than $50\%$ of adults support the tax increase.

*This problem is a good reminder to set the significance level at the beginning of a significance test. Different significance levels can produce different conclusions.*

###Answer

At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, they should conclude that more than $50\%$ of adults support the tax increase.

choices.content: At the $\alpha=0.01$ significance level, they should conclude that more than $50\%$ of adults support the tax increase.
At the $\alpha=0.01$ significance level, they should conclude that less than $50\%$ of adults support the tax increase.
At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, they should conclude that more than $50\%$ of adults support the tax increase.
At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, they should conclude that less than $50\%$ of adults support the tax increase.

assessment_items.question_content:
A poll from a previous year showed that $10\%$ of smartphone owners relied on their data plan as their primary form of internet access. Researchers were curious if that had changed, so they tested $H_0: p=10\%$ versus $H_\text{a}:p\neq10\%$, where $p$ is the proportion of smartphone owners who rely on their data plan as their primary form of internet access.

They surveyed a random sample of $500$ smartphone owners and found that $13\%$ of them relied on their data plan. The test statistic for these results was $z\approx 2.236$, and the corresponding P-value was approximately $0.025$.

**Assuming the conditions for inference were met, which of these is an appropriate conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

The P-value in this problem is approximately $\blueD{0.025}$.

If $\alpha=\purpleD{0.01}$, then we have $\blueD{0.025} \geq \purpleD{0.01}$, in which case they would fail to reject $H_0$.

If $\alpha=\goldD{0.05}$, then we have $\blueD{0.025} < \goldD{0.05}$, in which case they would reject $H_0$ and accept $H_\text{a}$.

In this context, accepting $H_\text{a}: p\neq10\%$ is equivalent to saying that the proportion has changed from $10\%$.

*This problem is a good reminder to set the significance level at the beginning of a significance test. Different significance levels can produce different conclusions.*

###Answer

At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, they should conclude that the proportion has changed from $10\%$.

choices.content: At the $\alpha=0.01$ significance level, they should conclude that the proportion has changed from $10\%$.
At the $\alpha=0.01$ significance level, they should conclude the proportion is still $10\%$.
At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, they should conclude that the proportion has changed from $10\%$.
At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, they should conclude the proportion is still $10\%$.

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a large poll in a previous year, about $80\%$ of homes in a certain county had access to high-speed internet. The following year, researchers wanted to test $H_0: p=0.8$ versus $H_\text{a}:p<0.8$, where $p$ is the proportion of homes in this county with high-speed internet access.

They took a random sample of $250$ homes from that county and found that $191$ of them had access to high-speed internet. The test statistic for these results was $z\approx -1.42$, and the corresponding P-value was approximately $0.077$.

**Assuming the conditions for inference were met, which of these is an appropriate conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

The P-value in this problem is $\blueD{0.077}$.

If $\alpha=\purpleD{0.05}$, then we have $\blueD{0.077} \geq \purpleD{0.05}$, in which case they would fail to reject $H_0$.

If $\alpha=\goldD{0.10}$, then we have $\blueD{0.077} < \goldD{0.10}$, in which case they would reject $H_0$ and accept $H_\text{a}$.

In this context, accepting $H_\text{a}: p<0.8$ is equivalent to saying that less than $80\%$ of homes in the county had access to high-speed internet.

*This problem is a good reminder to set the significance level at the beginning of a significance test. Different significance levels can produce different conclusions.*

###Answer

At the $\alpha=0.10$ significance level, they should conclude that less than $80\%$ of homes in the county had access to high-speed internet.

choices.content: At the $\alpha=0.10$ significance level, they should conclude that more than $80\%$ of homes in the county had access to high-speed internet.
At the $\alpha=0.10$ significance level, they should conclude that less than $80\%$ of homes in the county had access to high-speed internet.
At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, they should conclude that more than $80\%$ of homes in the county had access to high-speed internet.
At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, they should conclude that less than $80\%$ of homes in the county had access to high-speed internet.

assessment_items.question_content:
A campaign manager for a political candidate released a series of advertisements criticizing the opposing candidate in an upcoming election. The opposing candidate previously had the support of $45\%$ of voters, so the manager wants to test $H_0:p=0.45$ versus $H_\text{a}:p < 0.45$, where $p$ is the proportion of voters that support the opposing candidate.

After running the advertisements, the campaign manager obtained a random sample of $500$ voters and found that $220$ of those sampled supported the opposing candidate. The resulting test statistic was $z\approx-0.45$ and the P-value was approximately $0.33$.

**Assuming the conditions for inference were met, what is an appropriate conclusion at the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

Let's try that with the values from our situation.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{P-value}}&\stackrel{?}{<}\goldD{\alpha}\\\\
\blueD{0.33} &>\goldD{0.05}
\end{align}$

So they have failed to reject  $H_0: p=0.45$. They cannot accept $H_\text{a}:p<0.45$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Conclusion in context

Failing to reject $H_0: p=0.45$ says that our sample proportion of $\dfrac{220}{500}=44\%$—while lower than the hypothesized $45\%$—could plausibly happen by random chance alone when the true proportion is $45\%$. 

So we don't have enough evidence to conclude that less than $45\%$ of voters support the opposing candidate.

###Answer

They cannot conclude that less than $45\%$ of voters support the opposing candidate.

choices.content: They can conclude that less than $45\%$ of voters support the opposing candidate.
They can conclude that more than $45\%$ of voters support the opposing candidate.
They cannot conclude that less than $45\%$ of voters support the opposing candidate.
They can conclude that exactly $45\%$ of voters support the opposing candidate.

assessment_items.question_content:
An online retailer had a satisfaction guarantee, where they accepted a return and gave a refund for any reason within 30 days of a purchase. About $4\%$ of orders were returned under this policy. The company changed the timeframe to within 15 days, and they were curious if that would also change what proportion of orders were returned. They took a sample of orders to test $H_0: p=0.04$ versus $H_\text{a}: p\neq0.04$, 
where $p$ is the proportion of all orders that would result in a return under the new policy.

The sample of $500$ orders showed that $15$ orders were returned. Using these results, they calculated a test statistic of $z=-1.14$ and a P-value of approximately $0.25$.

**Assuming the conditions for inference were met, what is an appropriate conclusion at the $\alpha=0.10$ significance level?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

Let's try that with the values from our situation.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{P-value}}&\stackrel{?}{<}\goldD{\alpha}\\\\
\blueD{0.25} &>\goldD{0.05}
\end{align}$

So they have failed to reject  $H_0: p=0.04$. They cannot accept $H_\text{a}:p\neq0.04$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Conclusion in context

Failing to reject $H_0: p=0.04$ says that our sample return rate of $3\%$—while different than the hypothesized $4\%$—could plausibly happen by random chance alone when the true return rate is still $4\%$. 

So we don't have enough evidence to conclude that the return rate is no longer $4\%$.

###Answer

Fail to reject $H_0$. This isn't enough evidence to say that that the return rate is no longer $4\%.$

choices.content: Reject $H_0$. This is strong evidence that the return rate is no longer $4\%$.
Reject $H_0$. This isn't enough evidence to conclude that the return rate is no longer $4\%$.
Fail to reject $H_0$. This is strong evidence that the return rate is no longer $4\%$.
Fail to reject $H_0$. This isn't enough evidence to conclude that the return rate is no longer $4\%$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The mayor of a town read an article that claimed the national unemployment rate was $8\%$. They suspected that the unemployment rate was lower in their town, so they took a sample of $128$ residents to test $H_0: p=0.08$ versus $H_\text{a}:p<0.08$, where $p$ is the proportion of residents that are unemployed.

They found that $8$ residents in the sample were unemployed. Using these results, they calculated a test statistic of $z \approx -0.73$ and a P-value of approximately $0.23$.

**Assuming the conditions for inference were met, what is an appropriate conclusion at the $\alpha=0.10$ significance level?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

Let's try that with the values from our situation.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{P-value}}&\stackrel{?}{<}\goldD{\alpha}\\\\
\blueD{0.23} &>\goldD{0.10}
\end{align}$

So they have failed to reject  $H_0: p=0.08$. They cannot accept $H_\text{a}:p<0.08$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Conclusion in context

Failing to reject $H_0: p=0.08$ says that our sample proportion of $\dfrac{8}{128}\approx6\%$—while lower than the hypothesized $8\%$—could plausibly happen by random chance alone when the unemployment rate is still $8\%$. 

So we don't have enough evidence to conclude that the unemployment rate is lower than $8\%$.

###Answer

Fail to reject $H_0$. This isn't enough evidence to conclude that the town's unemployment rate is lower than $8\%$.

choices.content: Reject $H_0$. This is strong evidence that the town's unemployment rate is lower than $8\%$.
Reject $H_0$. This isn't enough evidence to conclude that the town's unemployment rate is lower than $8\%$.
Fail to reject $H_0$. This is strong evidence that the town's unemployment rate is lower than $8\%$.
Fail to reject $H_0$. This isn't enough evidence to conclude that the town's unemployment rate is lower than $8\%$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Antonia read an article that said $26\%$ of Americans can speak more than one language. She was curious if this figure was higher in her city, so she tested $H_0:p=0.26$ vs. $H_\text{a}:p>0.26$, where $p$ represents the proportion of people in her city that can speak more than one language.

Antonia took a sample of $120$ people in her city found that $35\%$ of those sampled could speak more than one language. The test statistic for these results was $z\approx 2.25$, and the corresponding P-value was approximately $0.01$. Assume that the conditions for inference were met.

**Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.05$ level to conclude that the proportion of people in her city that can speak more than one language is greater than $26\%$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

###Conclusion in context

The P-value was lower than the significance level, which says that the sample proportion of $35\%$ was [[☃ definition 1]] higher than $26\%$.

So Antonia should conclude that the proportion of people in her city that can speak more than one language is greater than $26\%$.

###Answer

Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.05$ level to conclude that the proportion of people in her city that can speak more than one language is greater than $26\%$?

**Yes, because $0.01<0.05$.**

choices.content: Yes, because $0.01<0.05$.
Yes, because $35\%>26\%$.
No, because $0.05>0.01$.
No, because $0.26>0.01$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A company that produces cranberries knows that about $7\%$ of its cranberries are bruised in the bagging process. They changed their bagging method, and they were curious if the proportion of cranberries bruised was different.

They tested $H_0: p=0.07$ versus $H_\text{a}:p\neq0.07$, where $p$ is the proportion of cranberries bruised, with a random sample of $600$ cranberries. They found that $10\%$ of cranberries in the sample were bruised. Those results yielded a test statistic of $z \approx 2.88$ and a P-value of approximately $0.004$. Assume that the conditions for inference were met.

**Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.01$ level to conclude that the proportion of cranberries bruised is different than $7\%$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

###Conclusion in context

The P-value was lower than the significance level, which says that the sample proportion of $10\%$ was [[☃ definition 1]] different than $7\%$.

So the company should conclude that the proportion of cranberries bruised is different than $7\%$.

###Answer

Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.01$ level to conclude that the proportion of cranberries bruised is different than $7\%$?

**Yes, because $0.004<0.01$.**

choices.content: Yes, because $10\%>7\%$.
Yes, because $0.004<0.01$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Omar is a quality control expert at a factory that paints car parts. He knew that $20\%$ of parts were being painted with an error, so he made a change in the painting process. After the change, he wanted to test $H_0:p=0.2$ versus $H_\text{a}:p < 0.2$, where $p$ is the proportion of parts that were being painted with an error.

Omar took a sample of $400$ parts and found that $16\%$ of them had an error. He used these results to calculate a test statistic of $z=-2.00$ and a P-value of approximately $0.023$. Assume that the conditions for inference were met.

**Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.05$ level to conclude that less than $20\%$ of the parts are being painted with an error?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

###Conclusion in context

The P-value was lower than the significance level, which says that the sample proportion of $16\%$ was [[☃ definition 1]] lower than $20\%$.

So Omar should conclude that less than $20\%$ of the parts are being painted with an error.

###Answer

Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.05$ level to conclude that less than $20\%$ of the parts are being painted with an error?

**Yes, because $0.023<0.05$.**

choices.content: Yes, because $0.023<0.05$.
Yes, because $16\%<20\%$.
No, because $0.16>0.05$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ava read an article claiming that $10\%$ of people in her county were senior citizens, and she wondered if this held true for residents in her city. She took a random sample of $225$ people from her city to test $H_0:p=0.10$ versus $H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.10$, where $p$ is the proportion of people in her city that are senior citizens.

Ava found that approximately $14\%$ of those sampled were senior citizens. She conducted an appropriate significance test, and the resulting test statistic was $z \approx 1.89$ with a corresponding P-value of approximately $0.06$. Assume that the conditions for inference were met.

**Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.05$ level to conclude that the proportion of people in her city that are senior citizens is not $10\%$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

Let's try that with the values from our situation.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{P-value}}&\stackrel{?}{<}\goldD{\alpha}\\\\
\blueD{0.06}&>\goldD{0.05}
\end{align}$

So Ava has failed to reject  $H_0: p=0.10$. She cannot accept $H_\text{a}:p\neq0.10$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Conclusion in context

Failing to reject $H_0: p=0.10$ says that the sample proportion of $14\%$—while different than the hypothesized $10\%$—could plausibly happen by random chance alone when the population proportion is actually $10\%$. 

So we **don't** have enough evidence to conclude that the proportion of people in her city that are senior citizens is not $10\%$.

###Answer

Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.05$ level to conclude that the proportion of people in her city that are senior citizens is not $10\%$?

**No, because $0.06>0.05$**

choices.content: Yes, because $0.10<0.14$.
Yes, because $0.06<0.14$.
No, because $0.06>0.05$
No, because $1.89>0.05$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A business knew that $30\%$ of its customers were less than $25$ years old. The business wanted to increase this percentage, so they created a marketing campaign that targeted this age group. After the campaign, the business obtained a random sample of $50$ customers to test $H_0:p=0.3$ versus $H_\text{a}:p > 0.3$, where $p$ is the proportion of this business's customers who are less than $25$ years old after the marketing campaign.

The business found that $36\%$ of those sampled were less than $25$ years old. They calculated a test statistic of $z\approx0.93$ a P-value of approximately $0.177$ for these results. Assume that the conditions for inference were met.

**Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.10$ level to conclude that the proportion of this business's customers who are less than $25$ years old is greater than $30\%$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

Let's try that with the values from our situation.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{P-value}}&\stackrel{?}{<}\goldD{\alpha}\\\\
\blueD{0.177}&>\goldD{0.10}
\end{align}$

So the business has failed to reject  $H_0: p=0.3$. They cannot accept $H_\text{a}:p>0.3$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Conclusion in context

Failing to reject $H_0: p=0.3$ says that the sample proportion of $36\%$—while higher than the hypothesized $30\%$—could plausibly happen by random chance alone when the population proportion is actually $30\%$. 

So we don't have enough evidence to conclude that the proportion of this business's customers who are less than $25$ years old is greater than $30\%$.

###Answer

Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.10$ level to conclude that the proportion of this business's customers who are less than $25$ years old is greater than $30\%$?

**No, because $0.177>0.10$.**

choices.content: Yes, because $0.177<0.36$.
Yes, because $0.36>0.3$.
No, because $0.177<0.3$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Adele read a report saying that $49\%$ of teachers in the United States were members of a labor union. She wanted to test whether this was true in her state, so she took a random sample of $300$ teachers from her state to test $H_0:p=0.49$ versus $H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.49$, where $p$ is the proportion of teachers in her state who are members of a labor union.

Adele found that $44\%$ of teachers in the sample were members of a labor union. She calculated a test statistic of $z \approx -1.73$ a P-value of approximately $0.083$ for these results. Assume that the conditions for inference were met.

**Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.05$ level to conclude that the proportion of teachers in her state that are members of a labor union is different than $49\%$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Reject vs. fail to reject $H_0$

$\begin{align}
&\text{P-value }< \alpha \rightarrow \text{reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{accept } H_a \\
&\text{P-value } \geq \alpha \rightarrow \text{fail to reject } H_0 \rightarrow \text{cannot accept } H_a
\end{align}$

Let's try that with the values from our situation.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\text{P-value}}&\stackrel{?}{<}\goldD{\alpha}\\\\
\blueD{0.083}&>\goldD{0.05}
\end{align}$

So Adele should fail to reject  $H_0: p=0.49$. She cannot accept $H_\text{a}:p\neq0.49$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Conclusion in context

Failing to reject $H_0: p=0.49$ says that the sample proportion of $44\%$—while different than the hypothesized $49\%$—could plausibly happen by random chance alone when the population proportion is actually $49\%$. 

So we don't have enough evidence to conclude that the proportion of teachers in her state that are members of a labor union is different than $49\%$.

###Answer

Is there sufficient evidence at the $\alpha=0.05$ level to conclude that the proportion of teachers in her state that are members of a labor union is different than $49\%$?

**No, because $0.083>0.05$.**

choices.content: Yes, because $0.083<0.49$.
Yes, because $0.44<0.49$.
